data.title: SouthState Bank: A Premium Sunbelt Franchise, But Macro Risks Keep Us On The Sidelines
data.description:
Thesis

SouthState Bank Corporation (SSB) is a growing regional bank that has demonstrated strong return metrics and sound balance sheet management, but mixed credit quality. The bank will likely continue to deliver solid performance in portfolios where it stands as a core position; however, the current macroeconomic environment and valuation of SSB make it a less than ideal time to enter into new positions in this regional bank. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on SSB.

Company Profile

SouthState Bank Corporation is headquartered in Winter Haven, FL, and provides financial services through its flagship subsidiary, SouthState Bank. The firm offers consumer, commercial, mortgage, and wealth management banking services. SouthState Bank operates in nine states, including Florida, Texas, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Colorado, Alabama, Virginia, and Tennessee. As of December 31, 2025, the company holds roughly $67 billion in assets and $55 billion in deposits.

Return Metrics

SSB has demonstrated above-average return metrics over the prior eight quarters, providing evidence that the firm’s strategy of organic expansion in high-growth markets is working. Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE) is one of the most common return metrics in banking. This metric shows the bank’s net income relative to its average tangible common equity. Tangible common equity removes intangible assets like goodwill, which can heavily distort return metrics. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has generated ROTCE in the double digits (note the dip in Q1, 2025 was a result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group). Additionally, the bank’s ROTCE remains north of the peer median of 15% as of Q4, 2025.

Net Interest Income (NII), arguably the most important return metric for a bank, measures the difference between the interest the bank receives on its assets (loans and securities) versus the interest it pays out on its liabilities (mostly deposits). Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has grown NII an impressive 64%. It is noted that the large increase in NII from Q4, 2024 to Q1, 2025 was the result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group.

Finally, net interest margin (NIM), another important return metric for banks, measures the net interest income generated versus the average earning assets (largely, loans and investments) used to obtain it. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has produced NIM in the 3% to 4% range, demonstrating the bank is making sound use of its assets to generate income; the peer median for Q4, 2025, was 3.65%.

Balance Sheet

Turning to the balance sheet, the Common Equity Tier 1 ratio (CET1) measures the firm’s highest quality capital versus its risk-weighted assets. This ratio tells us the degree to which the bank can absorb losses without needing external support. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has produced a CET1 ratio well above the 6.50% threshold needed to be considered “well-capitalized” by the U.S. Prompt Corrective Action framework. Note the dip in Q1, 2025 was caused by the acquisition of Independent Bank Group causing the denominator (risk-weighted assets) to rise by roughly $18.1 billion.

Additionally, the Tier 1 Leverage ratio is an additional metric that assesses a bank’s loss-absorbing capacity, except rather than using risk-weighted assets like the CET1 ratio, the Tier 1 Leverage ratio uses average total consolidated assets in the denominator, thus examining the equity cushion as a proportion of the bank’s total balance sheet. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has maintained a Tier 1 Leverage ratio well above the 5.0% threshold needed to be considered well-capitalized. Again, note the dip in Q1, 2025 as a result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group.

Credit Quality

SSB has demonstrated strong credit quality with the exception of one large write-off over the prior eight quarters. The Net Charge-Offs (NCO) to Total Average Loans ratio measures the proportion of loans written off by the bank versus the total average loans held on the balance sheet. This ratio has hovered mostly under 0.10% over the prior eight quarters. In other words, less than 1/10th of a percent of the bank’s loans are written off. Please note that the reported numbers for Q1, 2025, and Q2, 2025, were 0.38% and 0.21%, respectively, but these were a result of the charge-offs incurred due to the acquisition of Independent Bank Group. The ratio is being adjusted for these data points due to the acquired charge-offs not being representative of SouthState Bank’s management but rather Independent Bank Group’s management.

However, the jump in Q3, 2025 was due to one large credit loss of a commercial and industrial loan for $21.5 million. This charge-off is concerning as it was sudden and no prior reserve was set aside for it. Although management insists this was an isolated event with no similar credits in the portfolio, it does demonstrate that idiosyncratic events can bypass the early warning indicators set up by the bank.

An additional metric used to assess a bank’s credit quality is the ratio of the Allowance for Credit Losses (ACL) divided by the total Non-Performing Loans (NPL). This ratio tells us the cushion the bank has set aside for its troubled loans. The higher the ratio, the more the bank is able to withstand potential losses caused by these problem loans. SSB has consistently set aside roughly two times its balance of non-performing loans in allowance, which is a sound credit quality practice.

Valuation

For valuation, we will use price-to-tangible book value (P/TBV) and price-to-earnings (P/E) as backup metrics. P/TBV is the preferred metric for banks as it strips out intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise due to mergers and acquisitions rather than organic core operations. On a P/TBV level, SSB trades at a slight premium to the justified fair price when using the peer median P/TBV of 1.53 and the tangible book value of SSB of 56.27 as of Q4, 2025, which yields a justified price of $85.94. The current price of $88.18 sits at a 2.60% premium to the justified price.

On a P/E level, SSB trades at a discount to the justified price. An important note is that the P/E ratio can be heavily distorted in the banking industry due to an income statement item called the Provision for Credit Loss (PCL), which is a line item to account for defaulted loans. SSB trades at a 7.71% discount to the justified price of $95.55 when using the peer median P/E ratio of 9.67 and the most recent adjusted earnings per share of $2.47 as of Q4, 2025.

Taking the midpoint of the justified price using P/TBV and P/E, we arrive at a fair value of $96.07, which represents an 8.30% upside from the current price of $88.71 as of the time of this writing.

Macroeconomic Uncertainty

The current macroeconomic environment should be mentioned prior to making any recommendation on a cyclical firm like a regional bank. The heightened uncertainty from the Iran war, concerns around private credit, and the potential for stagflation (low growth, high inflation) make this a less than ideal time to enter into new positions on regional banks. Regional banks benefit from a steepening yield curve (short end falling, long end rising), which has been widely anticipated until recent months. The reason banks benefit from a steepening yield curve is because their funding costs from deposits are short-term in nature and fall when the short end of the yield curve falls. Conversely, banks’ assets are generally long-term in nature and provide higher yield when the long-end rises. If the Iran war, private credit, or a period of stagflation produces an inverted or flattening yield curve, this would harm SSB and regional banks in general. SSB is currently asset-sensitive, meaning it gains NII when rates rise and loses NII when rates fall. Although this will help counterbalance the negative effects of an unfavorable move in rates, the overall effect will still be adverse due to the macroeconomic shock.

Conclusion

SSB is an efficient financial institution with a solid footprint in rapidly growing markets of the United States. The bank has produced strong return metrics and maintains a healthy balance sheet, but the credit quality practices remain mixed. The current macroeconomic factors, including the war in Iran causing yield curve uncertainty, private credit concerns, and the potential for a stagflationary recession, make it a less than ideal time to enter into a new position at a regional bank, especially for the possibility of only an 8% return. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on SSB. I will reassess if the Fed engages in an aggressive rate-cutting cycle, geopolitical uncertainties resolve, or a more favorable price entry is provided.

data.title: What Active REIT Managers Bought And Sold In Q4 2025
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Landlord

As we have explained in the past, we don't make buying or selling decisions based on what any other investors are doing, including the best active REIT portfolio managers.

That said, we are very interested in what the REIT "smart money" is doing. It represents one data point among many that we consider in our research process.

Of course, most of the time, we do not have access to active REIT managers' commentary or thinking on why they made buy and sell decisions. This limits the value of seeing their quarterly trades. We can only guess, in most cases, about why any given active manager made the decisions they made during the last quarter.

However, some useful information and themes still emerge from poring over active REIT managers' 13F filings, so let's jump in and see what we can learn.

The latest filings show position sizes and trades as of the last quarter.

Starwood Capital Group Management

Billionaire Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group is mostly focused on direct property investments, but several years ago, the company began scooping up shares of deeply undervalued REITs and now maintains a small, concentrated REIT portfolio. Here is what he said in a past CNBC interview:

"By the way, when credit comes back, you are gonna see REITs take off... There are some unbelievable bargains in REITs. We did the same thing during the pandemic. We bought a dozen stocks all over the world and we had a 70% IRR on that stuff. We are already buying some stuff in the public market..." Barry Sternlicht, CEO/Chairman, Starwood

This small group of REITs gives a view into what Barry Sternlicht finds attractive on the public markets.

As you can see, Starwood's primary bet is on Sunbelt apartments, as over 2/3rds of the REIT portfolio is invested in the three big Sunbelt multifamily REITs: Camden Property Trust (CPT), Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), and NexPoint Realty Trust (NXRT).

In interviews as well as quarterly earnings conference calls, Sternlicht has repeatedly articulated his long-term bullishness on Sunbelt apartments based on the rapid drop in new supply over the next few years.

However, Starwood seems to be bullish on multifamily in general, given its exposure to Class A coastal apartment landlord Equity Residential (EQR) and Northeast-focused Veris Residential (VRE).

Lastly, we note that Starwood continues to hold one of our favorite industrial REITs, First Industrial Realty (FR).

Land & Buildings Investment Management

Jonathan Litt is an activist REIT investor and the founder of Land & Buildings.

Although we have at times disagreed with Litt in the past, we highly respect him as a thought leader in the public real estate space. Therefore, we pay close attention to what his investment company is buying and selling. While L&B does engage in shareholder activism for a number of its holdings, the company also seems to be a silent investor for others.

We note that, like Starwood, L&B's second-largest position is First Industrial (FR), which is also one of our largest holdings.

L&B's largest position is Centerspace (CSR), an owner of Class A and B apartments in the Mountain West region of the US (Colorado, Minnesota, Montana, North Dakota, and so on). This part of the country is typically overlooked by institutional investors and may hold hidden value. Perhaps L&B sees the potential for shareholder activism. That would explain its nearly 5% ownership stake in the REIT.

We note that L&B lightened up on its three senior housing REITs -- American Healthcare REIT (AHR), Ventas (VTR), and National Health Investors (NHI) -- in Q4 while adding to its positions in First Industrial, Equinix (EQIX), Sun Communities (SUI), American Homes 4 Rent (AMH), and Outfront Media (OUT), among others.

L&B continues buying Six Flags (FUN), but so far there are no signs that their quest to trigger a real estate spinoff is going to come to fruition. We will share our thoughts on the situation soon in a separate article here at High Yield Landlord.

Also, L&B opened a decent-sized position in National Storage Affiliates (NSA) in Q4, which has proven a smart move, as the stock price is up over 20% year-to-date as of February 18th.

L&B also opened a smaller position in Curbline Properties (CURB), a specialty retail REIT that owns small-format shopping centers with high frontage (visibility from the adjacent street).

In Q4, L&B also sold entirely out of their positions in Extra Space Storage (EXR), Crown Castle (CCI), Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT), AvalonBay Communities (AVB), and UDR Inc. (UDR).

Cohen & Steers

Cohen & Steers (CNS) is an asset management company focused on real assets, especially REITs, with $55.3 billion of assets under management.

Being such a large and well-respected specialist in publicly traded real estate securities, we naturally take a strong interest in how they structure their portfolio and what they're currently buying and selling.

Cohen & Steers' Top 20 Holdings:

Holdings #11-20 in the lower image above are interesting, because this mixture of mostly mid-cap REITs diverges from the passive index. We find that CNS added to blue-chip retail REITs like Kimco Realty (KIM) and Agree Realty (ADC) in Q4 while trimming Class A mall REIT Simon Property Group (SPG) and luxury resort landlord Host Hotels (HST).

Another interesting theme is that CNS doubled down on data center REITs Digital Realty (DLR), Equinix (EQIX), and Iron Mountain (IRM) on their dips during Q4, and they were also buying self-storage REITs like Extra Space Realty (EXR) and Public Storage (PSA).

But as you can see, Cohen & Steers' top 10 holdings don't stray terribly far from the largest market cap REITs in the passive index.

It's striking that top-tier senior housing REIT Welltower (WELL) accounts for over 11% of the total portfolio at a market value of $6.2 billion.

The sheer amount of capital under management by Cohen & Steers does constrain their top holdings to larger REITs. But the good news is that we know how CNS would structure a portfolio with much less capital because of its Real Estate Active ETF (CSRE), which has a mere $250 million in AUM.

Here are CSRE's top ten holdings:

With CSRE's ability to have large positions in smaller REITs, the ETF overweights National Storage Affiliates (NSA), Outfront Media (OUT), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), and Diamondrock Hospitality (DRH).

This reminds us of what Warren Buffett once said about how the size of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) limited its investable universe. If he had less AUM to work with, Buffett would have been able to invest more heavily in small- and mid-cap companies.

And, we might add, this is a benefit of being an individual investor. We are able to take advantage of attractive opportunities among smaller market-cap REITs quite easily.

Let's focus on CNS's biggest buys (by share count) in Q4.

Cohen & Steers' Top Buys in Q4:

We were intrigued to see that four of our holdings at High Yield Landlord were top buys for CNS during Q4: Kite Realty (KRG), Crown Castle (CCI), Kimco, and Rayonier (RYN).

Clearly, CNS remains bullish about high-quality and well-located open-air retail real estate, as we are.

They also seem optimistic about Crown Castle's US wireless tower portfolio as well as its simplification and deleveraging plans this year.

Lastly, CNS must be at least somewhat bullish on the cyclical timber industry because of its addition of Rayonier.

Now, moving on to the top sells/trims during the quarter.

Cohen & Steers' Top Sells in Q4:

It appears as though Cohen & Steers prefers Diamondrock Hospitality over Host Hotels right now, which indicates company-specific preferences rather than a macro view on the hospitality sector.

CNS also trimmed its position in single-family rental REIT Invitation Homes (INVH), perhaps related to political risks. We are happy to see that, though, because it contributed to a lower stock price and gave us an even better entry point for INVH's high-quality and highly scaled portfolio.

CNS also trimmed its American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) position, which could simply indicate tax-loss selling at year-end.

CNS also significantly trimmed its position in VICI Properties (VICI) in Q4, which was a theme for active REIT investment managers during the quarter. This was likely related to concerns about Las Vegas foot traffic, financial softness in the middle class, and rumors about a potential rent reset from one of VICI's major tenants.

So far, though, this feels like a "sell the rumor" situation. Given the fact that VICI made it through the COVID-19 pandemic without rent reductions (and while collecting 100% of contractual rent!), we doubt that the much milder headwinds facing gaming operators today will result in material damage to VICI.

CenterSquare Investment Management

CenterSquare is another commercial real estate investment management company with $12.8 billion of AUM in public securities.

Given its fairly large REIT portfolio, CenterSquare unsurprisingly shares a decent amount of overlap with Cohen & Steers' portfolio, with Welltower acting as an anchor.

CenterSquare's Top 20 Holdings:

A few themes emerge here.

First, CenterSquare is evidently bullish on senior housing, data centers, self-storage, high-end retail, and single-family rentals.

Second, CenterSquare exhibited bullishness across all of its top 20 holdings in Q4, which signifies optimism over most areas of commercial real estate.

Top buys by share count include several High Yield Landlord holdings.

CenterSquare's Top Buys in Q4:

Class A mall landlord Macerich (MAC) was the top buy by share count, while Healthcare Realty (HR), Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), and Kimco also show up in this list.

The long-term outlook for healthcare-related names like HR and ARE is fairly clear, given aging demographics. And increasing exposure to MAC was probably related to the REIT's low valuation, the new CEO's promising turnaround plan, and the portfolio's exposure to the strong side of the K-shaped economy.

On the other hand, CenterSquare did engage in significant trimming and sales in Q4.

CenterSquare's Top Sells In Q4:

Notably, the firm sold most of its position in Healthpeak Properties (DOC), which may be a vote of no-confidence in CEO Scott Brinker's capital allocation prowess. Management's decision to sell medical outpatient properties to double down on beleaguered life science real estate is a risk that the market has so far received poorly.

Likewise, CenterSquare sold out of NNN REIT (NNN) while significantly trimming Federal Realty (FRT), Rexford Industrial (REXR), and VICI Properties, perhaps due to tax-loss harvesting. Whether these sales reflect CenterSquare's view of the REITs' fundamentals or are merely temporary trades is unknown.

Baron Real Estate Income Fund (BRIIX)

Here is one more active REIT manager we like to monitor. The Baron Real Estate Income Fund is the second-highest rated real estate fund by Morningstar, trailing only the non-income-focused Baron Real Estate Fund (BREFX).

Part of the secret of the Baron real estate funds' success has been their freedom to invest in real estate-adjacent spaces like alternative asset management (Brookfield Corporation) and real estate services firms (Jones Lang LaSalle Inc.). We also do that at High Yield Landlord with positions in companies like IWG and HASI.

Most of the portfolio, however, is invested in REITs. As you can see above, the Baron fund is heavily concentrated in industry bellwethers Prologis (PLD) and Welltower. And in general, Baron appears bullish on industrial real estate, senior housing, high-end retail, data centers, and wireless towers.

During Q4, Baron increased its position in senior housing, self-storage, timberland, and hospitality.

Meanwhile, Baron decreased its positions (or sold out entirely) in REITs that exhibited price weakness in late 2025, indicating tax-loss harvesting may have played a role in these trades.

In fact, in management's commentary following the quarter, they expressly stated that trimming AMT and selling IRM were motivated by short-term factors and that they may buy these REITs again in 2026.

While the commentary did not cover why Baron trimmed VICI, we will note here that all the major active REIT investment managers trimmed their positions in this REIT during Q4.

Without knowing their reasoning behind this, however, we can't say for sure what motivated this exodus.

Takeaways

The first takeaway we have from this analysis is that there is disagreement about particular REITs even within the "smart money" aka active managers. That reinforces the idea that no one active manager's buying or selling decision is enough to be really meaningful.

The second takeaway is that the REIT "smart money" is broadly bullish on senior housing, data centers, and higher-end retail. And there appears to be some rising optimism about self-storage. (That optimism for self-storage has been rewarded so far in 2026.)

Also, Healthcare Realty is getting more love from active managers now that the REIT switched CEOs, reduced its dividend, and initiated a smart turnaround plan.

The third takeaway is that while most of the top sales in Q4 were unique cases, there are a few themes. Office and hospitality both saw outsized selling in Q4, and VICI Properties was widely trimmed by active managers as well.

Meanwhile, Baron Funds and Cohen & Steers were selling single-family rentals in Q4 while Land & Buildings and CenterSquare were buying SFR REITs.

At High Yield Landlord, we will continue to monitor what the best active REIT managers are doing as we manage our active portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street ended the week substantially lower as concerns about the conflict in the Middle East lingered and oil prices stayed higher at around $100 per barrel. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve announced its decision to keep interest rates unchanged, as widely expected.

Brent futures (CO1:COM) rose around 8.9% in the past five days to settle around $112 on Friday, while WTI Crude (CL1:COM) actually declined around 0.6% this week to around $98 per barrel. It is, however, an increase of almost 55% and 47%, respectively, from a month ago.

In addition, the Federal Reserve held interest rates steady at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting on Wednesday, in line with expectations. In its communication, the Fed cited the uncertainty of the Middle East conflict weighing on the economy.

In economic news, the February Producer Price Index came in hotter at +0.7% month-over-month, compared to the expected +0.3%, and Core PPI, excluding foods and energy, was +0.5% month-over-month vs. +0.3% consensus.

Moreover, U.S. Treasury yields rose significantly this week due to renewed concerns about the near-term path of monetary policy after the Fed’s decision to keep rates steady. The U.S. 10-Year bond yield (US10Y) was up 2.45% this week to 4.39%, while the U.S. 2-Year bond yield (US2Y) was up 4.8% to 3.91%, and the U.S. 30-Year yield (US30Y) was up 0.8% this week to 4.94%.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) lost -1.9%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped -2.1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -2.1%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: High Templar Tech: AI Pivot With A Hefty Cash Balance
data.description:
High Templar Tech Ltd (HTT), an enabler of AI-driven technology, is in a way trading far below where it would likely be, if not for the fact that it is currently in a gray zone as to what the future holds for the company. This state of affairs likely contributed to the recent decline in the stock, which has resulted in a loss of close to a third of its value in a couple of months. However, the decline has slowed down in recent days. This is worth noting because HTT is arguably undervalued according to at least one metric, although HTT probably needs to do a few things if the stock is to recoup prior losses.

HTT looks to make a new start

It is important to mention that HTT only started trading a few months ago in the place of what used to be known as Qudian with the ticker QD. Shares of the latter were delisted in December to be replaced by shares of the newly listed HTT. QD was engaged in various endeavors during its time, but most recently it was active in e-commerce with its last-mile delivery business. This business was wound down, and the new HTT is expected to focus on being an enabler of AI-driven technology.

Nevertheless, a previous article about the former QD from October 2025 written by myself went over the decision to wind down the last-mile delivery business and its impact. On the one hand, closing down meant QD would have to look for something else to do, which would likely require, among other things, start-up costs, as any new business requires. On the other hand, the delivery business was losing money, and while closing it down would incur costs, its absence was essentially a case of addition by subtraction. In the end, I decided to rate QD a buy in the article.

The chart above shows how HTT has struggled to gain traction after it started trading in December. The stock has drifted lower since it started trading in December, something that has continued into the new year. HTT is down 31.3% YTD in 2026, but the decline of the past several months may also have created an opportunity because with the decline in prices, valuations and the price to get in on HTT are also reduced.

What the first report from HTT had to say

It may have gotten drowned out by all the turmoil hitting the stock market in the wake of the war in Iran, but HTT released its first quarterly report since it began trading in December on March 11. This report showed how there is a cost associated with winding down a business, but it also suggests there is some value to be found in HTT, enough to draw at least a look.

HTT wound down the delivery business, and the most recent, or Q4 FY2025, report showed its impact, with revenue declining by 93.9% to just RMB3.16M, or $0.45M, down from RMB52.22M in Q4 FY2024. This led to a GAAP net loss attributable to shareholders of RMB163.1M, or $23.3M, in Q4 FY2025, which translates to a net loss of RMB1.03 or $0.15 per ADS, worse than the loss of RMB0.38 in Q4 FY2024.

If all the quarters are added up, then FY2025 revenue decreased by 81.1% YoY to RMB40.96M or $5.86M, down from RMB216.43M in FY2024. Yet HTT managed to finish with net income attributable to shareholders of RMB708.63M or $101.33M in FY2025, up from net income of RMB91.73M in FY2024, which converts to net income of RMB4.25 or $0.61 per ADS.

This partly reflects how HTT, or QD to be more exact, was bleeding cash from the money-losing last-mile delivery business, and the cash infusions it required to keep the business going, but the main reason why HTT was able to stay out of the red for the year as a whole was because it was able to offset losses from operations with non-operating income, including income from interest and investments. In the fourth quarter, non-operating income was affected by fluctuations in forex and other losses on investments, which is why the last quarter, unlike the three preceding quarters, finished with a loss.

Why HTT is worth taking into consideration

HTT would not have been able to offset losses if it did not have access to a large amount of assets on the balance sheet, which are used to generate income through, for instance, interest. While HTT did end FY2025 with short-term borrowings totaling $225.37M, this was more than offset by cash and cash equivalents totaling $791.12M, time and structured deposits totaling $269.59M and short-term investments totaling $125.73M.

In comparison, the current market cap of HTT stands at just $403M after the stock closed at $2.44 per ADS on March 20, which gives HTT the distinction of being valued at less than the cash, or its equivalent, on the books. The balance sheet also shows restricted cash and cash equivalents totaling $217.88M and longer-term investments totaling a combined $28.5M.

If everything is added up, then total assets amounted to $1,946.62M, far more than total liabilities of $238.32M, which results in HTT having a book value of about $1,708.3M, or $10.53 per ADS, since the total number of ADS/ADRs is about 165.1M. This means HTT is available at well below book value with a price-to-book of just 0.23x. Using this, it can be argued that HTT deserves a higher valuation than the one it has at the moment based on the state of the balance sheet.

Was the loss in Q4 FY2025 a one-off thing or the start of a new trend?

However, whether HTT is truly undervalued based on the balance sheet depends on what happens in the income statement. While FY2025 ended with a net profit, the last, or fourth, quarter incurred a loss. If HTT keeps losing money in subsequent quarters, then that will affect the balance sheet by eating into book value. Being valued at less than book value would then not be unwarranted in such a situation.

But the loss in the fourth quarter is unlikely to signal the start of persistent quarterly losses for a few reasons. First, HTT also recorded a loss in the final quarter of FY2024, only to be followed by profits in subsequent quarters. It has something to do with the end of the year when certain charges and costs come up. The results in Q4 FY2025 were also adversely affected by headwinds like exchange rates and other temporary hits like impairment charges.

HTT can still count on over a billion dollars in assets with which it can generate income that can offset the charges emanating from the shutting down of the delivery business. Keep in mind that this income from, for instance, interest is recurring, unlike the charges from the delivery business that will eventually go down to zero once everything has been shut down. Shutting down the delivery business is not a recurring event but something one and done.

HTT still earned $101.33M, or $0.61 per ADS, in FY2025, which accords HTT a P/E ratio of 4.0x with the stock at $2.44 per ADS, and this income was mostly the result of the assets on the books, something HTT still has access to. HTT may not be able to repeat this again in FY2026, but it also suggests HTT does not have to incur persistent quarterly losses like in Q4 FY2025 with all the income-generating assets it has at its disposal, especially with the adverse impact of the delivery business fading.

What needs to happen if the stock is to be set free

However, there is one issue that needs addressing if the stock is to stop drifting, and it concerns the future of HTT. While the cost associated with winding down the delivery business is finite and will end sooner or later, the same cannot be said of whatever HTT intends to do next, which is supposed to revolve around AI.

While HTT has lots of cash with which to fund any new business it wants to start, it is not infinite. HTT wants to move into AI, but it is not clear how much it will cost over the long run and/or whether it will be successful. It is not impossible for the AI business to turn into a repeat of the last-mile delivery business. That is something that requires constant spending without generating enough in return.

This does not mean the AI business cannot work out, but we just do not know, and that is an issue. Keep in mind that there is a lot of talk about AI and all the capex spending it entails for many companies. Many investors seem to be getting apprehensive about all the cash AI requires without actually showing what investors will get in return.

This apprehension may have affected HTT in terms of how the stock has traded recently, on top of other problems the stock market has been dealing with lately. If, for instance, HTT needs to keep pumping money into AI with no limit, then that will eventually cause the balance sheet to degrade to the point it will no longer look as pristine as it does right now. If HTT wants to get the stock moving in the right direction, it probably needs to provide some more clarity as to the future as it relates to AI.

Investor takeaways

If HTT wanted to unlock shareholder value, then a quick way to do it would be to offer to buy back all the outstanding shares at some premium to where it last closed at. At this time, the shares are trading at so far below book value, and below the cash available for that matter, that buying back the shares makes a lot of sense.

If HTT were to make such a decision, the stock would likely pop in response. There are about 165.1M shares out there, and at $2.44 each, HTT would need $403M, something it can easily afford with the assets it has, even if it pays a premium on top of that amount. However, no such decision has been made, and the stock has done rather poorly in 2026.

HTT looks cheap at current stock prices compared to what it has on the balance sheet, but the uncertainty as to how much HTT will have to spend on AI before it starts to generate meaningful returns overshadows it. The risk for HTT is that the AI business becomes a failed endeavor like the last-mile delivery business turned out to be. HTT will have then spent a lot of money for nothing, really. In the best case, HTT is able to make AI a success, which should cause the stock to rally from current depressed levels.

HTT would probably be wise to carefully step into the AI business and not go all in without any restraint. HTT should also not hesitate to pull out of the AI business if it feels things are not working out to prevent too big of a hit on the balance sheet. The stock would likely react well to such a decision because HTT seems to be trading at a major discount due to the uncertainty as to how much spending there needs to be in the future.

HTT can take things slowly because with all the assets on the books that generate income, it can afford to. It can wade into AI and pull out with not too much of a loss because there is no pressure on HTT to find something immediately to take the place of the delivery business. That is the advantage HTT has due to all the cash, or what can be converted into cash, on the balance sheet.

Longs may need to be patient with the stock, but I am bullish on HTT. HTT needs to provide more clarity on how it intends to go about things with regard to AI, but current valuations, which include valuing at half the cash and cash equivalents on the books, warrant long HTT. True, AI will need cash to get up and running, but it may not be as much as the market seems to fear, and HTT always has the option of turning its back on AI, just as it did with the delivery business.

HTT has shown it will not attempt to push a dead horse, as it did by shutting down the delivery business when HTT determined it could not make it work, so HTT deserves the benefit of the doubt that it will again make the right decision should AI prove too costly to be worth pursuing. HTT deserves a chance to show it can try something else while keeping the balance sheet in good shape.

data.title: Hydrograph: Demand Scaling And The Defense Angle
data.description:
This article is a follow-up to my first piece available here, I will thereafter assume you are familiar with it before reading this one. Today, I would like to cover and discuss the various significant developments that have happened since my previous article in November, and give a qualitative assessment of how HydroGraph (HGRAF) is tracking against the expected execution timeline I laid out previously.

I will first summarize what those significant developments have been, and then this will lead me to discuss my two main themes for today: How HydroGraph should be (and is) scaling up the end demand for its products, and how they can leverage qualification into the U.S. Defense ecosystem as an accelerator for said demand. I alluded to that angle in my last piece, but there are now some tangible early signs that HG is executing on that front, which look promising in my view and deserve further discussion.

Significant events and execution milestones

I will now list all the press released events since November 2025, review how they track against my expectations, and then dive deeper into our two topics. All of these PR will be relevant to the story.

December 9: HydroGraph Announces Appointment of James Baker to Technical Advisory Board

January 6: HydroGraph and GEIC Expand Collaboration through Tier 1 Membership

January 27: HydroGraph Announces Construction of Two Additional Hyperion Reactors to Expand Production Capacity

February 10: HydroGraph Qualifies Hubron International for Fractal Graphene(TM) Compounding Partner Program

February 23: HydroGraph Appoints John Neale as Chief Financial Officer

February 24: HydroGraph Receives US EPA and UK REACH, and EU REACH Regulatory Clearances for Commercial Scale Graphene Sales Activities

March 5: HydroGraph Announces Closing of its C$30 Million LIFE Offering

March 17: HydroGraph Certifies Broadway as Qualified Compounding Partner, Expanding Commercial Graphene Masterbatch Capabilities in the UK and Europe

Here is what my expected milestones were back in November:

Q1 2026: EPA approves both TSCA submissions. Hydrograph secures the other permits needed for the Houston facility (fire safety, etc.) and begins construction. They confirm the buildout of 2-4 more reactor units (in Kansas / Austin) which start producing at 10 tpa.

There hasn't been any disclosure about Houston construction yet, but the Austin HQ buildout (which I forgot to mention as a milestone, whoops) is reportedly done and online. Furthermore, their recent LIFE fundraising document/PR mentions the use of the proceeds to fund the Houston buildout. While HydroGraph itself has been quiet about their manufacturing plant and hasn't disclosed its acetylene partner yet, the collective wisdom of HG's retail investor base has unearthed some very relevant data point that shed some light on what is happening.

A job opening has recently appeared in Bellville, TX (in the Houston area) for a "Plant Manager to lead the planning, build-out, and launch of [a] new graphene manufacturing facility". Interesting. What's more interesting is that HydroGraph is registered as a member of the Bellville Chamber of Commerce, and that Bellville also happens to host an acetylene production plant from Western International Gas and Cylinders. A quick google search reveals that WIGC is "the world's largest acetylene cylinder filler". Have we found the location of the future HG plant? I bet we did. As the Plant Manager will be hired "before groundbreaking" we can deduce this hasn't begun yet but will likely soon after hiring. I had end of Q1 in mind but I'm not shocked about a Q2 construction start, which now looks to be the most likely outcome.

In any case, the most critical milestone since November was the EPA approval, and they absolutely delivered on that, even securing the UK and EU market clearance all at once, which I didn't expect. As I hadn't researched their efforts in those markets, I had a vague timeline in mind somewhere in 2026, but I'm glad they cleared all those hurdles. This is a strong signal, as they can now start transforming their 75+ prospective customers into volume sales over the medium term in most of the developed world. Clearly, the market understood the significance of that milestone, judging from the stock price action right before and after Feb 24. In the week prior, there were reports on Twitter from retail investors who contacted the EPA directly to ask about HGRAF submissions and reportedly got confirmation they were on track to be approved soon. This may have influenced the bullish price action prior to Feb 24 given that this is mostly a retail-driven stock at the moment, but this is only speculation on my part.

The USD stock rallied from a low of $2.13 on Feb 5, to $3.62 at close on Feb 23, to an intraday high and ATH of $4.57 on the day of the EPA/REACH press release, and a new ATH of $8.37 on Mar 11, before correcting down to about $5 as I'm writing this. What a wild ride in just a month. In my defense, I did previously warn you this stock had explosive volatility on both the up and downside, so I hope you buckled up.

Anyway, turning to the other expected milestone HG achieved in the quarter, the buildout of the 2 new Hyperion reactors shows they are executing on the supply side scale, which I covered in the previous article and is easily understood: build the Houston facility, build more reactors, profit. That is, if you can generate the demand for it. Which leads us to the demand side of the equation, and what is HydroGraph's playbook there.

Before I expand on this, I want to briefly cover the appointment of their new CFO, John Neale. He has experience in listed companies and this appointment should be seen as progress along the track for NASDAQ listing sometime this year, which I mentioned as a potential stock catalyst. He will be responsible for building the foundational financial processes and controls needed for a billion dollar public company.

Overall, as of the end of Q1 2026, HydroGraph is well meeting my expectations when it comes to execution, and I'm especially pleased to see they are rapidly moving in all directions in a strategically coherent way, which is no small feat for a company this size. They are securing funding, partners, regulatory clearance, production buildout and headcount ramp all at once, and that speaks to a good level of maturity for a company who is supposedly on the verge of explosive growth in the next few years.

Demand Side: Application scaling and the partner ecosystem

In order to sell tons of graphene, HydroGraph needs functionalized end products that offer superior performance in a cost-competitive manner versus the existing solutions, across the numerous segments of its TAM. What the end customers are buying is not graphene powder, it's:

  • A thermoset composite that offers superior thermal and UV resistance
  • Anti-corrosion coating for boats that lasts 5 times as long
  • Lubricants that require less consumption and improve machine lifespan or performance by X%
  • Concrete that is more resistant to earthquake, corrosion, thermal expansion, etc.

HydroGraph has a 75+ customer pipeline, reportedly. For each, they are going through the qualification cycle from customer requirements ("I need a thermoset with better thermal conductivity") to experiments to a working formulation answering said requirements ("With X% graphene loading in HDPE and Y process steps, we can produce a thermoset with Z% increase in thermal conductivity versus current best solution), to industrial trials, and eventually volume production. The process steps are roughly as follows :Lab proof of concept -> Application Validation -> Formulation -> Pilot manufacturing trial -> Qualification -> Contract Win -> Scale to volume production

For the first part of this process, the experimentation and formulation stage, this is done in collaboration and co-development with the customers' R&D teams, and the key organ on HG side for this R&D is the GEIC. This is why their upgrading to a Tier 1 membership with the GEIC is significant, as it signals a ramp-up of their R&D capacity and ability to accelerate the early qualification cycle, as well as generate new IP for graphene use cases.

The Tier 1 membership gives them a dedicated lab and a preferential access to the graphene experts from the GEIC and University of Manchester. The other key event here is the appointment of James Baker, former CEO of the GEIC, on their technical advisory board. His expertise from 12 years at the GEIC lies precisely in developing commercially viable projects from that early stage through the commercialization cycle, further enhancing HydroGraph's ability to execute on moving further through customer qualification.

I also can't resist pointing out that in the GEIC Tier 1 press release, HydroGraph mentioned specifically that it would deepen their partnership with the U.S Army Research Laboratory (ARL). We will come back to this and to James Baker, shortly.

After the R&D at the GEIC and starting at the formulation phase, the key to scaling end-demand faster is to build a partner ecosystem and embed yourself higher up the value and supply chain. If you're HydroGraph, you don't want to sell graphene powder, you want to sell the proprietary formulation of the end-product. For that, you need specific experts to partner with you, potentially co-develop that IP, market it, produce it, sell it, and develop further iterations of end-products, across all of the market segments that HydroGraph aims to cover.

You can see the early stage of that strategy with Hydrograph's recent announcement of its first "Qualified Compounding Partners" (QCP) that are Hubron and Broadway Colours, two UK companies specializing in polymer masterbatches.These are little plastic or resin pellets that carry HG's graphene in a concentrated form and can be used as additives blending in a polymer production process. In our fictitious thermoset example, the masterbatch would be a pellet formulation and concentration working for a specific plastic polymer, e.g. "Use this masterbatch at 0.3% weight concentration in HDPE polymer to increase thermal conductivity by X% vs baseline".

While the two firms are small (~GBP 50 million revenue a year for Hubron), they have experience and expertise integrating carbon products from GMPs to carbon nanotubes in polymer masterbatches. They can sell off-the-shelf SKUs, and help develop custom proprietary formulations in collaboration with HydroGraph (likely with an IP/value sharing arrangement), or do toll manufacturing. Importantly, HG will now leverage those companies' existing sales channels to accelerate market adoption of graphene end products. Overall, it helps HG iterate and go-to-market faster in the specific field of polymer composites.

An interesting tidbit to note here is that both Hubron and Broadway announced similar partnerships with Black Swan Graphene in 2024. The Hubron press release specifically mentions that Black Swan was to be a "preferred partner" for graphene masterbatch commercialization. That is certainly interesting considering HG's partnership, it would stand to reason this means HydroGraph displaced Black Swan as a preferred supplier. This is speculative, and I have no data to infer whether this is true at either Hubron or Broadway, but a simple look at Black Swan product datasheets shows me that this is a graphite feedstock company with a clearly subpar product, as discussed in my previous article, so I believe HG's graphene would most certainly show superior performance at lower loadings than this fake graphene.

In any case, what's significant about those two partnerships is the signal for HydroGraph's broader playbook for scaling demand, which will likely involve replicating this strategy across its end markets. Find specialized development and manufacturing partners in coatings, battery materials, composites, and any other TAM segment. Iterate faster with both customer-led and research-led product development, build IP and move up the value chain. Leverage partner ecosystem's product development and sales capabilities to expand and accelerate end-product adoption.

This is how you truly go from "we sold a few kilos of research samples" to "we have several hundred tons of annual contracted sales across multiple use cases", eventually. But I would like to point out that we are still very early in the story, and this won't happen overnight. Still, those are very encouraging early signs. If you recall, I pointed in my first article to thermosets and composites as the likely first source of volume sales for HydroGraph, with initial purchase orders pre-EPA clearance. With the EPA/REACH secured and masterbatch partners, the prospect of this revenue materializing in the next year is certainly increasing, and regardless of the actual initial revenue figure, the announcement of first volume production sales could be another potent catalyst for the stock price.

Overall, I am satisfied with HydroGraph's execution so far on the demand side. In the next few quarters, I would be pleased to see more partners added to this early ecosystem, especially in other sub-markets such as coatings (which I believe to be one of the strongest medium-term potential markets).

The Path to Defense supply chain qualification is open

The other key topic I'd like to discuss today is what I referred to in my previous article as "the Defense angle". I highlighted that the superior properties of graphene had numerous defense applications, and that for any US (or Western more generally) defense end-use, any graphene made out of China-dependent graphite feedstock would be a non-starter due to their dominance over the supply chain and potential export controls. Combined with HydroGraph's superior graphene quality and early/first mover advantage, this could present an enormous opportunity for HG to get qualified and entrenched within the defense ecosystem supply chain.

I have to say, I feel vindicated on that point already, because there are several interesting (and easy to miss) signs that the early stage of this defense qualification is happening as we speak. First, I will have to reference another video interview you can find on Youtube, entitled "Is Graphene The Next Big Boom? | Kjirstin Breure" from a fellow named Adam Taggart. There is a key direct quotes I want to unpack here from KB at the 22:50 mark:

We've been working with the (US) Army and the Navy for about two years, and the Army has expressed interest in building an innovation hub in Austin, and to our knowledge we're the only graphene company that's really made the cut. So they've been very selective ; we believe it will be called the Graphene Innovation Consortia, and I believe we will be there with other defense companies, battery producers, potentially... As we receive more information from the military we will update the market, but all very very good signs for us.

While there are no public press releases regarding a potential "Graphene Innovation Consortia" (GIC) for the moment, I will take this statement at face value; HydroGraph is now a billion dollar market cap company with the hopes of NASDAQ uplisting, and Ms. Breure would surely expose herself to severe legal repercussions if the statements she made in this interview were materially misleading or false. Earlier in the same interview she also mentioned HG was working with the Army on "Ballistics protection materials", which would typically fall into the umbrella of things that the Army Research Lab would develop.

Anecdotally, there is/was a "US Army Futures Command" headquartered in Austin, which recently merged in October 2025 into a new "US Army Transformation and Training Command", still Austin-based. As the name suggests, this Army Futures Command is particularly concerned with modernization efforts and innovative technologies ; it is clear that graphene would fit the bill here as a topic of interest to them.

There are several more important things to note here. One is that HG has been working not only with the Army, but also the Navy, which (to my knowledge) we had never heard about before. This is in my view very significant as I believe the medium-term SAM for HG with the Navy might actually be higher than with the Army.

Specifically, the Navy spends billions of dollars a year on anti-corrosion coatings for ships, subs and infrastructure alone, this would be a clear-cut use case where successful product qualification would open up many millions of dollars in revenue opportunity. I am sure this is not the only one, but I certainly see it as one juicy prospect in that market.

The other thing I find very significant besides this potential GIC, is the length of engagement with the U.S. military. Neither the Army nor the Navy would spend two years engaging with HydroGraph if they weren't seeing tangible, promising results, possibly across multiple end-uses. Of course this is speculative, but two years of Army engagement + indications they want to deepen the collaboration around a formalized defense innovation consortium (a fairly typical defense sector playbook for new technology developments) clearly signals high interest from the US DoD to move forward on developing and manufacturing graphene-enabled materials. The fact that HG is reportedly the only graphene supplier qualified proves the points I was making about their superior product quality and production process and the importance of being fully independent from the China supply chain.

If you are still not convinced that this "Defense Angle" is gaining momentum, I have a few more breadcrumbs for you. Remember James Baker, the GEIC CEO now turned technical board adviser for HG? Before the GEIC, he spent about 15 years at BAE Systems, one of the largest defense companies worldwide, and a Prime contractor into both the US and EU defense ecosystems. For 9 of those 15 years, he was Managing Director of BAE's "Advanced Technology Centre" in the UK, aka their research unit responsible for developing new technologies.

He most likely has a fine understanding of the end-to-end pathway by which you can turn HG's graphene powder into fully qualified, program-of-record advanced materials for the defense industry, the likely hurdles on the way there, not to mention an address book that could open some doors for initial discussions. His expertise in that regard will be invaluable for HydroGraph to navigate this complex universe. Here's another strange coincidence, if you go to the GEIC partner list page, you can find BAE systems listed there as a Tier 2 partner. What's the chance HydroGraph is or will be engaging with them? I'd say fairly likely. (By the way, I believe up until recently Hubron was also listed as a Tier 2 GEIC partner but they are not anymore, I do wonder what to make of it).

The last clear signal that HydroGraph is serious about making a play for the defense end markets, and is executing in that direction, lies in their careers page. They currently have an open position for a "Director, Business Development, Government and Defense". Since the job posting will not be online forever, I have made a screenshot of it so you can retain access to it in the future.

Given the listed responsibilities and the seniority level of this job, this is a clear signal that HydroGraph is ramping up into the qualification cycle with the DoD. You don't hire a Director with Top Secret clearance unless you're planning to get a return on that investment. The requirements for experience navigating the government procurement and grant programs also show that HG expects to secure funding to develop these military applications, which would partly derisk that side of the growth story.

Again, my conclusion here is the same as in the previous section: we are still very early, but the first signs are clearly encouraging and show the company is executing and making progress in the areas that matter. The qualification cycles for defense tend to be long, but that's highly variable depending on the target market. Armor composites for tanks, or jet fighter airframes? A decade if you're lucky. Lubricants or coatings, any kind of "additive" that can be easily switched out? Two years, maybe. It's hard to say what the timeline to revenue will be here until we know more about the applications, but this is why I'm excited about a potential Navy corrosion coating or similar application: it's in the sweet spot of good revenue potential, and comparatively fast qualification cycle.

Regardless of what the current applications under development are, clearly the long-term potential for graphene-enabled materials in the defense space is nearly bottomless. More than the revenue that will come from this foray into defense in the next few years, the opportunity for HG to entrench itself within the US defense ecosystem (and possibly the EU/UK as well) may well be the real prize here. Once you become the program-of-record graphene supplier across a broad array of uses (and especially if you own some of the application/process IP), you become extremely hard to displace. That's the kind of moat that could last decades, if you do get there.

Lastly, HG's progress in qualifying new use-cases for the military will also contribute to expanding their knowledge base to derive applications for the non-defense segment of the same markets (e.g. an anti-corrosion coating for the military could easily be reformulated for civilian demand) and building their IP moat.

Conclusion

HydroGraph is so far executing well against the expectations I laid out in my previous article, and the EPA/REACH clearance has certainly cleared up the path for the first commercial scale contracts over the next 12 months, leading me to reiterate my Buy rating for a long-term horizon investor.

The execution milestones over the next year are well understood on the production side: build the Houston facility, and then scale production linearly at 10 tpa per new Hyperion reactor built. On the demand side, we now have new observable milestones that could also serve as stock catalysts over 2026, in addition to the first volume-scale contracts. One would be HG improving the breadth and depth of its partner ecosystem across end markets, and the other is any official qualification with the U.S. defense apparatus (e.g., a PR announcing the creation of the GIC, or that HydroGraph received a grant from the Army Research Lab).

As for the risks to the thesis, operationally they have not changed from my last piece; in my view (and as long as HG continues to execute), the main risk you have to be aware of is valuation, and this is why I do not yet rate HG a "Strong Buy".

The point is, HydroGraph is currently impossible to value from a fundamental perspective. The long-term TAM is huge but highly uncertain, most applications of graphene haven't been discovered yet, and even when they are, their adoption curve remains a mystery, while the cash flows they will generate are far into the future in any case. That's why you've barely seen any numbers in this piece, there's not yet anything tangible to build a valuation framework on. For now, and until production scaling and first contracted revenue start "fleshing it out", this is a story stock. It trades purely on narrative and potential, mostly from an enthusiastic retail crowd believing this company might just have something revolutionary.

Nevertheless, valuation doesn't cease to matter just because it can't be quantified, and with such incredible volatility, it may be tempting to succumb to our own fear and greed impulses and make the wrong investment decision (buy at the top or sell at the bottom). I can't tell you what the fair value of HydroGraph is, nobody can (yet). You have to read the story for yourself, then look at the company market cap at that moment and decide whether that's a fair price for the potential you see. Personally, I find the story quite compelling, though not at any price. I have added to my initial position on the day of the EPA/REACH clearance announcement, as it significantly derisked the 2026 revenue/production ramp, and I will continue to follow this company with great attention.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alibaba: Shockingly Bad Q3, Yet Astoundingly Good Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The essential thesis of my last article on Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) was that China's general strategy, as embodied by BABA's own AI efforts, was focused more on task-oriented models that are smaller and open-sourced.

As the relatively new phenomenon of "agentic AI" rises to the top of the AI-use cases list, these smaller models offer more localization capabilities, with greater control over data security and at a must lower cost than AI agents running on LLMs and non-language models.

This has been one of the key drivers of BABA's success in recent quarters, but a more balanced base of growth drivers is now emerging, and it is what drives my upgrade today to a Strong Buy. The combined narrative of growing agentic AI adoption in the enterprise segment and the heavy investments in instant commerce are the anchoring pillars, with the stock's historically low valuation multiples being the icing on the cake.

Sustainable Growth in Key Areas

I should like to present some extracts from the Q3 2026 earnings conference call, the press release, the presentation, and the 6-K filing that evidence my case for a Strong Buy on BABA.

First up on the agenda is the elephant in the room - a mere 2 percent growth in revenues, a 57-percent drop in adjusted EBITA, a 74-percent decline in operating income, diluted EPADS down 67 percent, a 49-percent dip in operating cash flow, and a 71-percent wipeout of free cash flow.

The sudden sell-off triggered by what appeared to be a very poor quarter of profitability and cash flows is only indicative of heavy investments in its instant (quick) commerce and cloud businesses. This is not a deteriorating company; it is one that is sacrificing short-term profitability and cash flows for the sake of faster growth in two critical areas - China eCommerce and Cloud Intelligence Group.

And why not, indeed? BABA is currently sitting on $80 billion worth of liquidity, so the operating expenses that dragged down adjusted EBITA, net income, OCF and FCF are not exactly running the company into the ground. I think investors may have judged BABA too harshly on this front.

The second item I would like to discuss is growth in the "AI + Cloud" segment because I believe it will be a much stronger driver of overall topline growth in the future. A 36-percent year-on-year growth rate might look small in the context of its current size of RMB43 billion versus China eCommerce's RMB159 billion, but it is not dissimilar to Amazon (AMZN) reporting $36 billion in AWS revenues against a much higher $127 billion coming from North America retail revenue for Q4 2025 reported last month. Incidentally, AWS revenue only increased by 24 percent year-on year, so Alibaba Cloud's infrastructure and AI offerings growing by 36 percent is certainly something to take note of.

The third agenda item for today is the investment in quick commerce. These efforts are already bearing fruit, and with the Qwen app's integration with Taobao Instant Commerce (rebranded from Ele.me) in January, we should see the needle moving even more meaningfully in Q4 2026. Per the 6-K filing:

we executed our plan to further grow the scale of our quick commerce business, improve user experience and enhance operating efficiency, with increasing efforts in high-value food orders and non-food categories. The quick commerce business continued to improve unit economics and increase average order value month-over-month during the quarter, driven by fulfillment logistics efficiency enhancement, order mix optimization, and strong customer retention.

This is what I like to see - investments going directly into growth drivers. The fact that the market sent the ADSes careening down from $135 dangerously close to $120, a little above which it stands as of this writing, looks to me as though the forest was missed for the trees.

The Rise of Agentic AI in the Context of BABA's $100 Billion Goal for Cloud and AI

The investments in quick commerce may take a few quarters to fully realize, and that is one long-term upside aspect investors should take note of. The real growth, per my strong view, will come from the consumption of AI though agentic models.

I said this over four months ago:

...BABA is effectively creating an ecosystem where smaller language models thrive. This is not to say LLMs are going the way of the dodo bird, but these derivatives, in my opinion, are the real problem solvers. They address specific use cases that are a) easily monetizable and b) power- and thereby cost-efficient even when run on more basic compute hardware.

What I did not make explicit, however, is precisely what Reuters reported two days ago, just before BABA's Q3 release. This quote is from a former BABA employee Brian Wong, who authored "The Tao of Alibaba":

"Think of it like having OpenAI, Amazon, Stripe, Uber, DoorDash, Ticketmaster, Expedia, Netflix and Charles Schwab all integrated into one ​text box you can just use natural language to execute," he said. "This is what the company has enabled through its restructuring and it's happening first in China. I don't see this happening in the U.S. because of ‌the challenges ⁠of integrating different platforms from different companies."

I say again, China's forced strategic direction for AI is what BABA is executing at the enterprise and consumer level. Of course, one would be correct to say that Qwen runs on a full range - from device-and-edge-based small to medium models and large cloud-deployed LLMs, but rapid adoption and mass consumption will come from app users, primarily. The enterprise workloads will provide more revenue visibility and predictability, but here is the reality of it:

Qwen models, the world’s most widely used open-source model family, surpassed 1 billion cumulative downloads on Hugging Face as of January 21, 2026.

The rapidity of adoption is astounding:

Since launching its Chinese New Year campaign on February 6, Qwen app has seen tremendous user engagement. By the end of February, approximately 140 million users have had their first AI-driven shopping experience through Qwen app’s agentic features from ordering food and groceries to ticketing and travel bookings. In February, consumer-facing Qwen has surpassed 300 million monthly active users across all platforms.

Indeed, the Qwen app is seeing such a seismic shift in the way consumers are engaging with Alibaba's myriad platforms. In the Reuters report linked above was this little nugget:

In February, an early push ​to get users to try Qwen’s new functions encountered some hurdles. Alibaba launched the first phase of a 3 billion yuan ($435.7 million) coupon campaign that allowed users to make in-app purchases on Alibaba-owned retail platforms using only chatbot prompts. The coupons proved too popular, prompting a temporary shutdown of the app.

I believe that is a wonderful problem to have in any business. Do remember that all of this is post-Q3, which means meaningful gains are on the table for Q4 and into FY 2027. This Qwen integration will play a major role in BABA's recovery story, not only as far as its stocks and ADRs are concerned, but more critically, a recovery to strong growth at the top and bottom lines.

Is BABA Pricey?

Valuation is a tricky minefield to traverse, because with the right metrics, one could argue either way. A bull might point to the price to book or equity value being 16 percent below the sector median, while a bear might point to the obvious weak spot - adjusted earnings multiples that represent a 50 percent to 60 percent premium over the sector.

A fair argument here would be that BABA is not an inventory-heavy business the way AMZN is. Indeed, BABA reports zero inventory, while AMZN reports $38 billion for that line item. Nor is BABA a capital-heavy one, with $36.4 billion in PP&E versus AMZN's $443 billion. That would imply that the bull side's argument is weak because a cheaper book value multiple is not material to the business.

The problem with earnings multiples is that it is not quite representative of the evolutionary shift that BABA is going through. Investments in quick commerce may be ongoing, and that is bound to put some pressure on OpEx, or adjusted EBITA, but these investments are as critical as the ones on the technology side. The new-found revenue and engagement thrusters that agentic AI represents to BABA are reliant on these investments.

It is a cycle, and I think investors are looking at the investment phase of the cycle and translating it into a lack of growth and profitability. Growth has slowed, undoubtedly, and the macro pressure from the housing debacle still persists with consumers. This is largely out of BABA's control, but ironically, it makes its AI strategy even more powerful. Even in China's still-struggling economy, consumers are more than willing to consume agentic AI with gusto. The penetration is still miniscule compared to the opportunity:

Alibaba’s sprawling business now encompasses everything from consumer-facing apps like Taobao and Tmall to cloud computing to enterprise AI agents.

And it’s the latter two segments that Alibaba is counting on to drive a lot of growth in the near future. Specifically, Alibaba is projecting that revenue from its AI and cloud computing divisions will hit $100 billion over the next five years.

Remember the current size of the business? It was RMB43 billion in Q3. Extrapolated to RMB172 billion as an annualized run-rate, that is still under $25 billion. A $100 billion cloud and AI business in the next five years would represent a CAGR of 32 percent.

This is what one should be valuing in any BABA financial model. The segment is already growing a little above that level at 36 percent, so I do not see $100 billion by 2030 to be unrealistic or even unachievable.

On the enterprise side of things, this is what CEO Eddie Yongming Wu had to say on the Q3 call:

as model-driven agents begin to handle mainstream work tasks across industries, our total addressable market will expand by several multiples.

This TAM expansion is precisely what will drive the $100 billion in annual revenues from just the AI and Cloud business.

What I see with the bear arguments for still-expensive earnings and sales multiples will gradually be diluted by growth on both lines, and if the price of the ADRs and stocks do not go up in the immediate future, it would only be a good thing for BABA bulls because the potential upside keeps getting bigger.

The risks are there, of course, and one should not ignore them. U.S. investors' perception of Chinese companies has long been far from positive, and particularly now because tariff and export control implications from both sides still continue to represent a significant overhang.

The surge in P/E ratio (forward) over the past year came from technology and eCommerce investments putting pressure on the bottom line, but its P/S (forward) multiple has contracted over the past three years, indicating that the incremental gains in sales were not enough to support stronger share price growth. To be fair, being up 20 percent over a three-year-period is not a bad investment, but one sees where the drag is coming from - and why these metrics are insufficient to paint a more accurate valuation picture.

The value is certainly present, but it is hiding behind ugly multiples that do not accurately reflect BABA's trajectory over the next five years. My Strong Buy is caveated with risk considerations, of course, so a word of caution about not overweighting it in your portfolio is most definitely called for. However, this need not be a speculative position, either. It is a firm view based on real-world developments that will capably support a long-term growth runway.

At least 38 analysts are bullish on BABA, with an average price target of $196, or 60 percent upside, but I would draw attention to the lower end, which is already above the current ADR price of $122. I think a Strong Buy is not an unrealistic rating; hence, my upgrade.

data.title: Starbucks Is Getting Its Glow-Up, But Risks Remain
data.description:
All good things take time: that’s the message that Brian Niccol, the Starbucks (SBUX) CEO who is credited with turning around Chipotle (CMG), is trying to tell investors who have soured on the coffee giant’s stock. Now a little over a year into his tenure, it’s difficult to argue with the fact that Niccol has made his stamp on the company known and implemented many changes, culminating in his “Back To Starbucks” plan with a goal of restoring coffeehouse vibes, reinstating the daily Starbucks habit, simplifying operations and removing discounting.

Starbucks’ fiscal Q1 (December quarter) results, reported in late January, is the first indication we have of Niccol’s plan providing green shoots. Shares of Starbucks have rallied modestly since then, though the stock remains down ~7% over the past year.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Starbucks in January, which came after the heels of when Starbucks decided to partially divest of its China business segment (a strategic decision I continue to view in a positive light). My principal concern on Starbucks had been, and remains lagging US performance, and with the return to positive comps in Q1, I’m more positive on Starbucks’ prospects. Still, the specter of a rich valuation and operating risks ahead still color my opinion here: so while I’m directionally more positive on Starbucks post-earnings, I’m reiterating my neutral rating for now. I think Starbucks belongs on your watch list and bought on major dips, but I think there are a lot of open questions and issues that need to be resolved until I can comfortably buy this stock and sleep soundly at night with it in my portfolio.

At current share prices, I see a balanced bull and bear case for Starbucks. On the positive side for the company:

  • Starbucks’ global footprint is unmatched. For better or worse, Starbucks has become the go-to coffee in many markets, and despite recent sales and macro headwinds the company has a global base of loyal customers
  • High gross margin business. Coffee is a structurally highly profitable business, despite recent increases in green coffee prices due to supply shocks. At high volume, Starbucks’ ability to scale its profitability is immense.

At the same time, however, we do acknowledge a number of critical risks the company faces:

  • Competition abounds. Starbucks is the world’s “default” coffee, and in many cases that can be a bad thing. Discerning customers opt for more local “third wave” coffee shops that offer higher quality roasts, while ascendant chains like Dutch Bros (BROS) that focus on drive through print better sales comps.
  • Frequent labor issues. Starbucks has powerful workers unions and the company is frequently in a position of being paralyzed by walkouts.
  • Waning operating margins. Rising labor costs and rents, on a weakening comp sales base, have eroded Starbucks’ natural gross margin advantages and led to declines in operating margin that need to be solved before the stock can truly rebound.

All in all, Starbucks remains a stock to watch closely: but it’s not the right moment to buy the dip just yet.

Q1 recap

Let’s now go through Starbucks’ latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 (December) results are shown in the chart below:

Starbucks’ revenue in Q1 grew 5.5% y/y to $9.92 billion, widely beating Wall Street’s mark of $9.66 billion (+2.8% y/y) by a significant 270bps margin, a meaningful upside surprise for a company that has seemingly only had bad news to share for the past few years. Also, growth accelerated 170bps versus the company’s 3.8% growth pace in Q4.

The most meaningful driver of sales growth was an improvement in U.S. sales comps, which is by far Starbucks’ most important market (U.S. revenue at 69% of overall company top line is more than two thirds of the business). Comp sales, which Starbucks defines as stores open continuously for at least the trailing 13 months, improved to 4% growth in Q1, buoyed by a 3% jump in transactions and a 1% benefit from pricing and order mix. This was a marked improvement from a -2% decline in Q4 (-4% in transactions, partially offset by a 2% bump in price). The large swing in traffic trends (a 7 point sequential acceleration) is by far the loudest indicator of a potential turnaround in the works.

This sequential improvement is the main catalyst that has me much more hopeful on Starbucks’ prospects. That said, there is underlying context that is important to understand before reading into these results too quickly. The first is that Starbucks is starting to face easier comps as well. The second is that Starbucks optically benefits from its recent closure of 500+ stores as part of the “Back to Starbucks” plan, which happened largely in Q4 and Q1. This reflects roughly 3% of Starbucks’ store base, and it’s fairly safe to assume these were likely the weakest performing stores of Starbucks’ portfolio. We don’t disagree with Niccol’s decision to close underperforming and unprofitable locations (which may be cannibalizing another Starbucks in the exact same town), but we also note that part of the sequential improvement in comps is due to this one-time removal of weaker stores, rather than fully due improvement in the stores that remain. Still; we are impressed that Starbucks posted 3% revenue growth in the U.S., despite a -1% shrinkage in store portfolio.

Internationally, the company’s results are less ambiguous. Here, we can fully get behind strong 5% comps growth (3% transaction growth, 2% price benefit):

We're also optimistic that Starbucks is now guiding to exceeding at least 3% comp sales growth in both the U.S. and globally. Additionally, the company is back to a growth mindset, with a plan to open 600-650 net-new locations in FY26 (just shy of 2% annual location growth, relative to opening the year with ~41k global stores).

Where we do remain cautious is on profitability, as we already previously noted. Despite top-line growth, Starbucks' operating income declined -21% y/y to $890.8 million: or a 9.0% margin that declined -290bps y/y, with the company absorbing additional operating costs and coffeehouse investments related to the "Back to Starbucks plan." The company has a tough hill to climb here, especially against rising labor costs: and Starbucks will need to prove that it can return to margin expansion before its stock can fully recover.

Valuation and key takeaways
